title: The man who counts birds for the city
summary: Explain a simple, low-tech way a city keeps track of its wildlife, and why someone does it by hand.
topic: A simple way a city watches its wildlife
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences explaining how someone in your city counts or watches animals, birds, or plants in a simple, low-cost way. Describe what they do, how often, and why it helps. Use present simple, adverbs of frequency, and the connector 'because'.
hintStarters: Every morning before work, a man in my neighbourhood…
He does not use any machine; he only uses…
The city pays him a little because…
I used to think it was a strange job, but now I…
sampleText: Every morning at six, an old man named Mr Park walks around the small pond near my flat in Seoul and counts the birds. He carries an old notebook and a pair of cheap binoculars, nothing else. He does not use a phone or a camera. He stops at the same five spots, waits a few minutes, and writes down how many birds he sees and what kind. He always starts at the north corner because the ducks rest there in the early light. Mr Park has done this for nineteen years, and he sends his numbers to the city office twice a month. At first this sounds like a strange way to study nature, but his notebooks tell a clear story. The city can see that some birds come earlier than they used to, and that one kind of duck has almost stopped coming. A camera could take photos, but it cannot tell you which birds are healthy or which ones are afraid of people. Mr Park can, because he watches them slowly and he is patient. He told me the work is cheap and useful, and that anyone with good eyes and time can do it. I asked if he ever wanted a day off. He smiled and said the birds do not take days off, so neither does he.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Adverbs of frequency with present simple
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'He always starts at the north corner' đặt 'always' trước động từ chính để nói tần suất. Trong tiếng Anh, các trạng từ như always, often, never thường đứng trước động từ thường (nhưng sau động từ 'to be'). Điều này hợp với thì hiện tại đơn dùng cho thói quen.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentence 'He always starts at the north corner' places 'always' before the main verb to show frequency. In English, adverbs like always, often, and never usually go before the main verb (but after the verb 'to be'). This fits the present simple used for habits.

sampleTranslationVi: Mỗi sáng lúc sáu giờ, một ông già tên Park đi quanh cái ao nhỏ gần căn hộ của tôi ở Seoul và đếm chim. Ông mang một cuốn sổ cũ và một cặp ống nhòm rẻ tiền, không gì khác. Ông không dùng điện thoại hay máy ảnh. Ông dừng ở năm điểm giống nhau, đợi vài phút, rồi ghi lại số chim ông thấy và loại nào. Ông luôn bắt đầu ở góc bắc vì lũ vịt nghỉ ở đó trong ánh sáng sớm. Ông Park đã làm việc này mười chín năm, và ông gửi các con số cho văn phòng thành phố hai lần một tháng. Thoạt nghe, đây có vẻ là cách kỳ lạ để nghiên cứu thiên nhiên, nhưng những cuốn sổ của ông kể một câu chuyện rõ ràng. Thành phố có thể thấy rằng một số loài chim đến sớm hơn trước kia, và một loài vịt gần như đã ngừng đến. Một máy ảnh có thể chụp ảnh, nhưng nó không thể cho bạn biết con chim nào khỏe mạnh hay con nào sợ người. Ông Park thì biết, vì ông quan sát chúng chậm rãi và ông kiên nhẫn. Ông nói với tôi công việc rẻ và hữu ích, và rằng bất kỳ ai có mắt tốt và thời gian đều có thể làm. Tôi hỏi liệu ông có bao giờ muốn nghỉ một ngày. Ông cười và nói lũ chim không nghỉ ngày nào, nên ông cũng không nghỉ.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best-written sentence about a daily habit.
options: Every morning at six, he walk around the pond and count the birds.
Every morning at six, he walks around the pond and counts the birds.
Every morning at six, he is walking around the pond and counts the birds.
Every morning at six, he walks around the pond and is counting the birds.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present simple 'walks' and 'counts' fit a daily habit. Option 2 drops the third-person 's'; options 3 and 4 misuse the continuous for a routine.
mcQuestions.hint: For a repeated daily action, do we use present simple or present continuous?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence with the adverb of frequency in the correct place.
options: He starts always at the north corner.
Always he starts at the north corner of every day.
He always starts at the north corner.
He start always at the north corner.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Always' goes before the main verb 'starts'. 'Starts always' is awkward; the third is unnatural; 'He start' drops the third-person 's'.
mcQuestions.hint: Where do adverbs like 'always' usually go: before or after the main verb?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence using 'because' to give a reason.
options: He starts there because of the ducks rest in the early light.
He starts there, so the ducks rest in the early light.
He starts there because the ducks resting in the early light.
He starts there because the ducks rest in the early light.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Because + clause' is correct. 'Because of' needs a noun; 'so' shows a result, not a reason; 'resting' is not a full verb here.
mcQuestions.hint: Does 'because' or 'because of' come before a full clause?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the most natural sentence about how often he reports.
options: He sends his numbers to the city office twice a month.
He sends his numbers to the city office two times in a month.
He sends his numbers to the city office twice per a month.
He sends his numbers at the city office twice a month.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Twice a month' is the natural phrase. 'Two times in a month' is clumsy; 'per a month' is wrong; 'at the city office' uses the wrong preposition for sending.
mcQuestions.hint: Which is more natural for frequency: 'twice a month' or 'two times in a month'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best sentence comparing birds now and before.
options: Some birds come more early than they used to.
Some birds come earlier than they used to.
Some birds come earlier as they used to.
Some birds come earlier than they use to.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Earlier than they used to' is correct. 'More early' is wrong (use 'earlier'); 'earlier as' should be 'than'; 'use to' drops the 'd'.
mcQuestions.hint: Is the comparative of 'early' formed with '-er' or with 'more'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the best sentence about what a camera cannot do.
options: A camera cannot to tell you which birds are healthy.
A camera cannot tell you which birds is healthy.
A camera cannot tell you which birds are healthy.
A camera can not telling you which birds are healthy.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Cannot tell' uses the base verb after the modal. Option 2 adds a wrong 'to'; option 3 has subject-verb disagreement ('birds is'); option 4 uses '-ing' after the modal.
mcQuestions.hint: After a modal like 'cannot', what form of the verb comes next?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about how he watches.
options: He watches them slow and he is patient.
He watches them slowly and he is patiently.
He watches them slowly and he has patient.
He watches them slowly and he is patient.
mcQuestions.explanation: Adverb 'slowly' and adjective 'patient' after 'is' are correct. 'Slow' is an adjective; 'patiently' is an adverb after 'is'; 'has patient' is the wrong verb.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'is', do we use an adjective or an adverb?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the best sentence describing his simple tools.
options: He carries an old notebook and a pair of cheap binoculars.
He carries an old notebook and a pair of cheaps binoculars.
He carries a old notebook and a pair of cheap binoculars.
He carries an old notebook and a cheap pair of binocular.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A pair of cheap binoculars' is correct. 'Cheaps' adds a wrong plural to the adjective; 'a old' should be 'an old'; 'binocular' should stay plural.
mcQuestions.hint: Do adjectives like 'cheap' take a plural '-s'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about who can do the work.
options: Anyone with good eyes and time can does it.
Anyone with good eyes and time can do it.
Anyone with good eyes and time can to do it.
Anyone who has good eyes and time can do them.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Can do it' uses the base verb after the modal and the right object. Option 2 adds '-es'; option 3 adds 'to'; option 4 uses plural 'them' for the singular task.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'can', do we use 'do', 'does', or 'to do'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the best closing sentence about not taking a day off.
options: The birds do not take days off, so neither he does.
The birds do not take days off, so also he doesn't.
The birds do not take days off, so neither does he.
The birds don't take days off, so neither do he.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'So neither does he' is the correct inverted form. Option 2 puts the words in the wrong order; option 3 is unnatural; option 4 uses 'do' instead of 'does'.
mcQuestions.hint: In 'so neither ___ he', does the helping verb come before or after 'he'?

criticalThinkingQuestion: When is a slow, human way of doing a job better than a faster machine? What might a person notice that a camera or sensor never will?
